---
title: "Part I — The Mathematics"
description: "A ten-week mathematical-economics bootcamp."
---

The first ten weeks build the mathematical apparatus you will need to read mid-twentieth-century mathematical Marxian economics, the planning literature, and the controversies in Part II without skipping equations.

The arc is cumulative. Each week leans on the previous one and feeds the next; the bridge sentences at the end of every chapter make the dependency explicit. If you find yourself stuck, the prerequisite checklist in [Appendix A](../appendices/appendix-a-prereq.qmd) is the place to back up to.

## The arc

1. **Multivariable calculus** — partial derivatives, gradients, the geometry of the optimum.
2. **Constrained optimization** — Lagrange multipliers and the Cobb-Douglas demo.
3. **Linear algebra** — vectors, matrices, the column picture.
4. **Eigenvalues and eigenvectors** — diagonalization, the spectral story.
5. **Economic optimization** — production sets, profit maximization, cost minimization.
6. **Linear programming and duality** — the simplex method and the dual.
7. **Input-output analysis** — Leontief's matrix, $(I - A)^{-1}$, sectoral interdependence.
8. **Planning models** — labor-time accounting, multi-sector planning under constraint.
9. **Differential equations** — linear systems, phase portraits, equilibria.
10. **Growth and stability** — von Neumann growth, Goodwin cycles, stability of the planned economy.

By Sunday of Week 10 you should be able to crack open Morishima's *Marx's Economics: A Dual Theory of Value and Growth* (1973) and follow Chapter 1 as math, not as ideology.

## What comes after

[Post-course](../post-course.qmd) gives a reading ladder into the trilogy and the planning literature; [Part II](../controversies/part-ii.qmd) puts the math you just built up against seven of the disputes that define the field.
